questions.question: What is the primary purpose of an `enum`?
options: To assign readable text names to integer constants to make code self-documenting
To define variables that change automatically
questions.answer: To assign readable text names to integer constants to make code self-documenting

questions.question: In `enum TrafficLight { RED, YELLOW, GREEN };`, what integer value does `RED` default to?
If `enum State { START=5, RUNNING, STOPPED };`, what is the value of `RUNNING`?
What is the primary physical difference between a `struct` and a `union`?
options: A struct allocates memory for every member individually; a union allocates one shared memory block used by all its members simultaneously
Structs hold ints; unions hold floats
questions.answer: A struct allocates memory for every member individually; a union allocates one shared memory block used by all its members simultaneously

questions.question: How many values from a union can you safely store and read at any one exact moment?
questions.answer: Only one

questions.question: If you write an `int` to a union, and immediately try to read the `float` variable from that same union, what happens?
options: It converts the int to float
You read corrupted/garbage data because the float interpretation of that int's binary memory is mathematically nonsensical
questions.answer: You read corrupted/garbage data because the float interpretation of that int's binary memory is mathematically nonsensical

questions.question: Can you use standard `typedef` with an `enum` or `union` like you do with structs?
questions.answer: Yes

questions.question: What happens if two items in an enum are manually assigned the exact same integer value?
options: It is completely valid; both names will represent the same constant value
questions.answer: It is completely valid; both names will represent the same constant value

questions.question: Which of these perfectly creates an enum variable?
questions.answer: enum Day today = MONDAY;

questions.question: Is memory allocated permanently for an `enum` definition itself?
options: No, enums are essentially compile-time constants (replaced with integers by the compiler)
questions.answer: No, enums are essentially compile-time constants (replaced with integers by the compiler)

sublevels.theory:
The **Size of a Union** is determined by its largest member.
If a union contains an `int` (4 bytes), a `double` (8 bytes), and a `char str[20]` (20 bytes), the physical size of the entire union is exactly 20 bytes. (Perhaps with a slight pad to align it to a 4 or 8-byte boundary).

Why use Unions? Memory Savings!
Often used in embedded systems or complex structs representing abstract "Nodes" or "Events". If an Event struct represents either a `MouseClick` or a `KeyPress`, you create a `union` inside the struct holding both. Because an event can only ever visibly be ONE of those actions, joining them in a union saves massive amounts of memory compared to a struct where both exist uselessly at the same time.

questions.question: How is the total memory size of a union calculated?
options: The size of its single largest member data type (plus possible padding)
questions.answer: The size of its single largest member data type (plus possible padding)

questions.question: If `union Test { int a; char b; double c; };`, what is the `sizeof(union Test)` assuming standard sizing?
Are unions inherently Type-Safe in C?
options: Yes, the compiler tracks what you wrote last
No, C provides zero tracking of what was last written. Reading the wrong union member causes silent logic bugs.
questions.answer: No, C provides zero tracking of what was last written. Reading the wrong union member causes silent logic bugs.

questions.question: What is an 'Anonymous Union'?
options: A union declared inside a struct without a name, allowing its members to be accessed directly as if they belonged to the parent struct
questions.answer: A union declared inside a struct without a name, allowing its members to be accessed directly as if they belonged to the parent struct

questions.question: How do developers generally solve the safety issue of not knowing which union variable is currently active?
options: By wrapping the union inside a struct alongside an `enum` Tag/Type tracker variable that indicates what data type is currently stored inside the union
There is no way to solve it
questions.answer: By wrapping the union inside a struct alongside an `enum` Tag/Type tracker variable that indicates what data type is currently stored inside the union

questions.question: What operator do you use to access a member of a union through a pointer `union Data *p`?
questions.answer: -> (Arrow)

questions.question: Can an enum value realistically be negative in C?
options: Yes, `enum { START = -5 };` is perfectly legal since enums are backed by signed integers.
questions.answer: Yes, `enum { START = -5 };` is perfectly legal since enums are backed by signed integers.

questions.question: If you initialize a union: `union Test t = {10};`, what happens?
options: It assigns 10 strictly to the VERY FIRST element defined in the union
questions.answer: It assigns 10 strictly to the VERY FIRST element defined in the union

questions.question: Can you declare a union that contains a struct, and vice-versa?
options: Yes, standard C allows arbitrary nesting of structs and unions
questions.answer: Yes, standard C allows arbitrary nesting of structs and unions

questions.question: Why are unions incredibly common in Network Packets or low-level Hardware Registers?
options: They allow you to treat the exact same block of raw memory bytes as different data types on the fly (e.g., viewing an int as 4 separate bytes)
They compress the data over WiFi
questions.answer: They allow you to treat the exact same block of raw memory bytes as different data types on the fly (e.g., viewing an int as 4 separate bytes)

sublevels.theory:
**Type Punning via Unions**
Type Punning is a tricky technique used to subvert the type system. If you take a float, you usually can't read its raw internal binary bits using bitwise `&` operators directly. 
However, if you put that `float` into a union with an `int`, you can write the Float, and immediately read the Int. This isn't a conversion—it's reading the *exact raw binary IEEE-754 representation* of the float as if it were a literal integer.

While strict aliasing rules in C make casting through pointers `*(int*)&float_val` technically Undefined Behavior, modern standards specifically grant an exception allowing type punning via `union` reading!

*(Note: The famous Inverse Square Root algorithm from Quake III Arena used this exact pointer-casting type punning trick to manipulate float bits!)*

questions.question: What is 'Type Punning'?
options: Circumventing the type system to treat the raw memory bits of one data type directly as if it were a different data type
questions.answer: Circumventing the type system to treat the raw memory bits of one data type directly as if it were a different data type

questions.question: Why is utilizing a `union` typically the safest, standard-compliant way to perform type punning in C?
options: Reading a different union member than what was written is explicitly allowed by C99 standards, sidestepping Strict Aliasing rules
questions.answer: Reading a different union member than what was written is explicitly allowed by C99 standards, sidestepping Strict Aliasing rules

questions.question: What is the 'Strict Aliasing' Rule in C?
options: Global vars must be named uniquely
Pointers of completely unrelated types (like int* and float*) are assumed by the compiler's optimizer to NEVER point to the exact same memory address
questions.answer: Pointers of completely unrelated types (like int* and float*) are assumed by the compiler's optimizer to NEVER point to the exact same memory address

questions.question: What horrible consequence can happen if you violate Strict Aliasing using wild pointer casts (e.g. `*(int*)&float_var`)?
options: The compiler's optimizer may reorder or delete your instructions because it assumes your pointers don't overlap, causing insane runtime logic bugs
questions.answer: The compiler's optimizer may reorder or delete your instructions because it assumes your pointers don't overlap, causing insane runtime logic bugs

questions.question: Can the compiler choose the backing integer size of an `enum`?
options: Yes, the C standard allows the compiler to use char, short, or int depending on the largest value defined in the enum
questions.answer: Yes, the C standard allows the compiler to use char, short, or int depending on the largest value defined in the enum

questions.question: What is an Endianness issue when reading structs/unions?
options: Different CPU architectures (Big-Endian vs Little-Endian) store multi-byte integers in reversed byte orders, making raw memory sharing across networks broken
questions.answer: Different CPU architectures (Big-Endian vs Little-Endian) store multi-byte integers in reversed byte orders, making raw memory sharing across networks broken

questions.question: If you have `union { unsigned int i; unsigned char c[4]; }`, what happens if you write to `i` and read `c[0]` on a Little-Endian CPU?
options: You get the MOST significant byte
You get the LEAST significant byte of the integer
questions.answer: You get the LEAST significant byte of the integer

questions.question: Are you allowed to check if two unions are equal natively using `union1 == union2`?
options: No, C does not support direct comparison operators on structs or unions
questions.answer: No, C does not support direct comparison operators on structs or unions

questions.question: How are Bit-Fields combined with Unions in hardware programming?
options: A union can contain a struct of bit-fields alongside a standard integer, allowing devs to read/write specific hardware bits OR wipe the entire register at once via the integer member
questions.answer: A union can contain a struct of bit-fields alongside a standard integer, allowing devs to read/write specific hardware bits OR wipe the entire register at once via the integer member

questions.question: What does standard C99 named-initializer syntax look like for a union?
questions.answer: union Test t = { .f = 3.14 };
